entities.name: OpenAI
entities.how_described: 本集把它说成：一个极其追求速度和雄心、采用真正自下而上组织架构的 AI 研究公司，内部用 Codex 极大加速了各种产品的开发。

evidence.quote: Codex is OpenAI's coding agent.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Being at OpenAI, I can't not ask about how far you think we are from AGI.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: Today, my guest is Alexander Embiricos, product lead for Codex, OpenAI's incredibly popular and powerful coding agent.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: In the words of Nick Turley, head of ChatGPT and former podcast guest, "Alex is one of my all time favorite humans I've ever worked with, and bringing him and his company into OpenAI ended up being one of the best decisions we've ever made."
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: Similarly, Kevin Weil, OpenAI's CPO, said, "Alex is simply the best."
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: In our conversation, we chat about what it's truly like to build product at OpenAI, how Codex allowed the Sora team to ship the Sora app, which became the number one app in the app store in under one month.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: What do OpenAI, Cursor, Perplexity, Vercel, Plaid, and hundreds of other winning companies have in common?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: I want to start with your time at OpenAI.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: I imagine OpenAI is very different from every other place you've worked.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: Let me just ask you this, what is most different about how OpenAI operates and what's something that you've learned there that you think you're going to take with you wherever you go, assuming you ever leave?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: By far, I would say the speed and ambition of working at OpenAI are just dramatically more than what I can imagine.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: But I have to say, working in OpenAI just made me reimagine what that even means.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: But I think one thing I've realized at OpenAI is the amount of impact that we can have and, in fact, need to have to do a good job is so high that I have to be way more ruthless with how I spend my time now.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Before we get to Codex, is there a way that they've structured the org or, I don't know, the way that OpenAI operates that allows the team to move this quickly?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: I think everyone likes to say that they're bottoms up, or at least a lot of people do, but OpenAI is truly, truly bottoms up.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Someone I've learned a ton from working here is a research lead, and he likes to say that at OpenAI, we can have really good conversations about something that's a year plus from now, and there's a lot of ambiguity in what will happen, but that's a right sort of timeline.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So I think the way that OpenAI runs, you can't read this or listen to a podcast and be like, "I'm just going to deploy this to my company."
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And Codex is OpenAI's coding agent.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I think this is critically important to achieve the mission of OpenAI, which is to deliver the benefits of AGI to all humanity.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: We have this strong sort of mission here at OpenAI basically to build AGI.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And it was quite interesting because we dogfood product a ton at OpenAI.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so Codex has been accelerating OpenAI over the course of the entire year, and the cloud product was a massive accelerant to the company as well.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: It just turns out that this was one of those places where the signal we got from dogfooding is a little bit different from the signal you get from the general market because at OpenAI, we train reasoning models all day and so we're very used to this kind of prompting and think upfront, run things massively in parallel and it would take some time and then come back to it later asynchronously.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And I could see how everyone at OpenAI is living very far in the future, and sometimes that won't work for everyone.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: So I think what we need to build as OpenAI is basically this idea of you have Chat, ChatGPT and not as a tool that's ubiquitously available to everyone, you start using it even outside of work to just help you.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I think the other really interesting thing is if we think about what is the most successful AI product to date, I would argue, it's funny actually not to confuse things at all, but the first time we used the brand Codex at OpenAI was actually the model powering GitHub Copilot.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So you can create this mixed initiative system that's contextually responding to what you're attempting to do.So in my mind, this is a really interesting thing for us as OpenAI as we're building.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: What has it done to the way you operate and the way PMs operate at OpenAI?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And I feel like I made some PMs at OpenAI sad.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: But a bit of the narrative here for me just to tell my story a bit was I was working on this screen sharing, pair programming startup, and then we joined OpenAI.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Being at OpenAI, I can't not ask about your AGI timeline and how far you think we are from AGI.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: And I think I would just ask yourself the question, "Hey, let's say I were to join OpenAI and work on Codex over the next six months and crush it, what does the life of a software engineer look like then?"
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Feels like it's completely infiltrated everything at OpenAI, which makes sense, that tracks.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

entities.name: Codex

evidence.quote: You lead work on Codex.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: Codex is OpenAI's coding agent.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Starting to see glimpses of the future where we're actually starting to have Codex be on call for its own training.

evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So we have a Codex code review that's catching a lot of mistakes.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: One of our major goals with Codex is to get to proactivity.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: When you think about progress on Codex, I imagine you have a bunch of evals and there's all these public benchmarks.

evidence.quote: Today, my guest is Alexander Embiricos, product lead for Codex, OpenAI's incredibly popular and powerful coding agent.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: Also, the 20x growth Codex is seeing right now and what they did to make it so good at coding, why his team is now focused on making it easier to review code, not just write code, his AGI timelines, his thoughts on when AI agents will actually be really useful, and so much more.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: The most obvious thing that comes to mind is just the explosive growth of Codex itself.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Before we get to Codex, is there a way that they've structured the org or, I don't know, the way that OpenAI operates that allows the team to move this quickly?

evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And Codex is OpenAI's coding agent.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And when you do so, you can then basically pair with Codex to answer questions about code, write code, run tests, execute code, and do a bunch of the work in that thick middle section of the software development lifecycle, which is all about writing code that you're going to get into production.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: More broadly, we think of Codex as what it currently is just the beginning of a software engineering teammate.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so we think of this as proactivity and one of our major goals with Codex is to get to proactivity.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so a large part of our goal with Codex is to figure out what is the shape of an actual teammate agent that is helpful by default.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Is that a kind of a differentiator in the way you think about Codex?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: Is there any stats, any numbers you can share about how Codex is doing?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: Yeah, Codex has been growing absolutely explosively since the launch of GPT-5 back in August.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Also, the Codex models are serving many trillions of tokens a week now, and it's basically our most served coding model.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: One of the really cool things that we've seen is that the way that we decided to set up the Codex team was to build a really tightly integrated product and research team that are iterating on the model and the harness together.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so we've reached a point where actually the Codex model is the most served coding model in the API as well.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And then all of a sudden Codex comes around.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: I think the tweet was the gnarliest bugs that he runs into that he just spends hours trying to figure out nothing else has solved, he gives it to Codex, lets it run for an hour and it solves it.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So the way that most people discover, the vast majority of users discover Codex today is either they download an IDE extension or they run it in their CLI and the agent works there with you on your computer interactively.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So what I'm hearing is the initial version of Codex was almost too far in the future.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And that tweet you were saying Karpathy made about, "Hey, give this your gnarliest bugs," obviously there's a ton going on in the market right now, but Codex-Max is definitely carrying that mantle of us tackling the hardest bugs.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So for instance, one of the things that we're really proud of is you can have GPT-5.1-Codex-Max work for really long periods of time.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So shipping this compaction feature that now just made this behavior possible to anyone using Codex actually meant working across all three things.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So the way that we built Codex is that it just uses the shell, but in order to make that safer and secure, we have a sandbox that the model is used to operating in.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And it's interesting that there's that approach to the super assistant and then there's this Codex approach.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: So I think that what we're doing with Codex is we're building a software engineering teammate, and as part of that, we're kind of building an agent that can use a computer by writing code.

evidence.quote: It's quite early, but we're starting to see people who are using Codex for coding adjacent product purposes.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: If you're going to have an agent capability to validate work, and let's say you have... I'm thinking of Codex Web right now, you have a pain that sort of reflects the work the agent did, what do you see first?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: That's actually one of the ways when people ask, "Hey, how do you run Codex on a really long task?"
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So this is like Tinder meets TikTok meets Codex.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: I think the other really interesting thing is if we think about what is the most successful AI product to date, I would argue, it's funny actually not to confuse things at all, but the first time we used the brand Codex at OpenAI was actually the model powering GitHub Copilot.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so we decided to reuse that brand recently because it's just so good, Codex, code execution.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Codex, code execution, did not know that.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: We have a Slack integration for Codex.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: Again, just ask Codex for help with that.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I think something that, I don't think it's super surprising, but something that's slightly surprising is we see... We're mostly building Codex to write code that's going to be deployed to production, but actually we see a lot of throwaway code written with Codex now.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: If I want to understand something, it's like, okay, just give Codex a bunch of data, but then ask it to build an interactive data viewer for this data.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Similarly, I've seen some pretty cool prototypes on our design team about if you want to... Well, a designer basically wanted to build an animation, and this is the Coin Animation Codex, and it was like normally it'd be too annoying to program this animation.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So they do a ton of product work and they actually have an entire vibe coded side prototype of the Codex app.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: We recently shipped the Sora Android app, and that was one of the most mind-blowing examples of acceleration, actually, because usage of Codex internally at OpenAI is obviously really, really high, but it's been growing over the course of the year, both in terms of now it's basically all technical staff use it, but even the intensity and know how of how to make the most of coding agents has gone up by a ton.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: It went from zero to launch to employees, and then 10 days later, so 28 days total, we went to just GA, to the public, and that was done just with the help of Codex.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I would say it was a little bit... I don't want to say easy mode, but there is one thing that Codex is really good at if you're a company that's building software on multiple platforms, so you've already figured out some of the underlying APIs or systems, asking Codex to port things over is really effective because it has something you can go look at.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so the engineers on that team were basically having Codex go look at the iOS app, produce plans of work that needed to be done, and then go implement those.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And basically we got to the point where that team has a ton of power users of Codex right now, and it got to the point they where basically... We were talking to them about it, because a lot of those engineers are people I used to work with before at my startup.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So the team now is ramping up on Windows and they're helping us make Codex better on Windows too, which is admittedly earlier, just the model we shipped last week is the first model that natively understands PowerShell.

evidence.quote: So yeah, it's been really awesome to see the whole company getting accelerated by Codex from... And most obviously, also research and improving how quickly we train models and how well we do it.

evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: Sora
entities.how_described: 本集把它说成：OpenAI 推出的应用。其 Android 版在极少数工程师借助 Codex 的情况下，仅用 28 天就开发完成并面向公众发布，拿下了应用商店排行榜第一名。

evidence.quote: One of the most mind-blowing examples of acceleration, the Sora Android app, like a fully new app, we built it in 18 days and then 10 days later, so 28 days total, we went to the public.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: In our conversation, we chat about what it's truly like to build product at OpenAI, how Codex allowed the Sora team to ship the Sora app, which became the number one app in the app store in under one month.

evidence.quote: And then the Sora video, okay, so I see how this all connects now.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: We recently shipped the Sora Android app, and that was one of the most mind-blowing examples of acceleration, actually, because usage of Codex internally at OpenAI is obviously really, really high, but it's been growing over the course of the year, both in terms of now it's basically all technical staff use it, but even the intensity and know how of how to make the most of coding agents has gone up by a ton.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so the Sora Android app, a fully new app, we built it in 18 days.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Could Sora have been built by, I don't know, a PM or designer?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

entities.name: Atlas
entities.how_described: 本集把它说成：OpenAI 开发的浏览器产品。团队重度使用 Codex 开发，将原本需要两三个工程师两三周的工作量骤降到一个工程师一周。它旨在通过掌握渲染引擎来为智能体提供第一手的上下文。

evidence.quote: And if you become an annual subscriber of my newsletter, you get a year free of 19 incredible products, including a year free of Devin, Lovable, Replit, Bolt, n8n, Linear, Superhuman, Descript, Wispr Flow, Gamma, Perplexity, Warp, Granola, Magic Patterns, Raycast, ChatPRD, Mobbin, PostHog, and Stripe Atlas.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: So for instance, when I think about launching a browser, which we did with Atlas, in my mind, one of the really interesting things we can then do is we can then contextually surface ways that we can help you as you're going about your day.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I want to talk about Atlas.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: And then Atlas was the other one that I think Ben did a podcast, the engine lead on Atlas, sharing a little bit about how we built there.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Atlas is actually... I mean, it's a browser, and building a browser is really hard.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And what's quite cool is that we shipped Atlas on Mac first, but now we're working on the Windows version.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And then Atlas you said took a week to build?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: So Atlas, not the whole week, but Atlas was a really meaty project.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so I was talking to one of the engineers on Atlas about just what they use Codex for.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And it's like, "Okay, now we've set it up so they can execute the build and it can see the results of its own changes, but we haven't yet built a good integration harness so that it can," in the case of Atlas... By the way, I don't know if they've done any of this or not.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So interestingly, Atlas, I want to talk about that briefly.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: You guys launched Atlas.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: I tweeted actually that I tried Atlas and then I don't love the AI only search experience.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: And I saw someone tweet, "Okay, we have Atlas now," which I imagine was always part of the plan.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: So I worked on Atlas for a bit.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: For example, I'm thinking of an engineer who was working on Atlas, since we were talking about it, he set up Codex so that it can verify its own work, which is a little bit non-trivial because of the nature of the Atlas project.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: 智能体 (agent)
entities.how_described: 本集把它说成：未来能自主操作计算机、具备主动性和上下文感知能力的 AI 系统。嘉宾认为，构建任何智能体的最佳方式可能就是先构建一个编码智能体，让模型通过编写代码来使用计算机。

evidence.quote: Codex is OpenAI's coding agent.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so we're kind of getting to this idea where if you want to build any agent, maybe you should be building a coding agent.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Today, my guest is Alexander Embiricos, product lead for Codex, OpenAI's incredibly popular and powerful coding agent.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: This episode is brought to you by Fin, the number one AI agent for customer service.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: Fin is the highest performing AI agent on the market with a 65% average resolution rate.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: No other AI agent performs better.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: And Codex is OpenAI's coding agent.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so a large part of our goal with Codex is to figure out what is the shape of an actual teammate agent that is helpful by default.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So the way that most people discover, the vast majority of users discover Codex today is either they download an IDE extension or they run it in their CLI and the agent works there with you on your computer interactively.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So if the agent needs to do something, it needs to run a command, it can do so within the sandbox.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: It's like a remote in the cloud agent that's coding for you asynchronously.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: But I will say it's like some of how we're thinking about this is evolving a little bit from being like, "Yeah, we're just going to think about the model and let's just train the best model," to really thinking about what is an agent actually overall?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And I'm not going to try to define agent exactly, but at least the stack that we think of it as having is it's like you have this model, really smart reasoning model that knows how to do a specific kind of task really well, so we can talk about how we make that possible.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So we're kind of getting to this idea where, well, if you want to build any agent, maybe you should be building a coding agent and maybe to the user, a non-technical user, they won't even know they're using a coding agent, the same way that no one thinks about are they using the internet or not, which is they're more just like, "Is WiFi on?"
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So I think that what we're doing with Codex is we're building a software engineering teammate, and as part of that, we're kind of building an agent that can use a computer by writing code.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so as that develops, I think we'll just naturally see that, oh, it turns out we should just always have the agent write code if there is a coding way to solve a problem instead of... Even if you're doing a financial analysis, maybe write some code for that.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: In my mind, just coding is a core competency of any agent including ChatGPT.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Because one very reductive view we could have for an agent is it's just going to be given a computer and it's just going to point and click and go around.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And then how we get there is difficult to chart a path because a lot of the questions around building agents aren't like, "Can the agent do it?"
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: But it's more about, "Well, how can we help the agent understand the context that it's working in?"
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: They probably want certain deterministic guarantees about what the agent can or cannot do.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Or they want to know that the agent understands this detail.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so we start to get to this thing where, yeah, we have this agent sitting in front of a computer, but we need to make that configurable for the team or for the user and let them... Stuff that the agent does often, we probably just want to build in as a competency that this agent has that it can do.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So I think we end up with this generalizable thing, that you were saying, of an agent that can just write its own scripts for whatever it wants to do.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: But I think that the really key part here is can we make it so that everything that the agent has to do often or that it does well, we can just remember and store so that the agent doesn't have to write a script for that again?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I think there's two sides to it, but the one we were just talking about is this idea that maybe every agent should actually use code and be a coding agent.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I think, to give an example right now, nowadays when you work with a coding agent, it writes a ton of code, but it turns out writing code is actually one of the most fun parts of software engineering for many software engineers.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And I would argue that reviewing agent written code is a place that today is less fun.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Another thing we could do is we can make it so that the agent's better able to validate its work.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: If you're going to have an agent capability to validate work, and let's say you have... I'm thinking of Codex Web right now, you have a pain that sort of reflects the work the agent did, what do you see first?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And one terrible idea for how it could look is that actually there's a mobile app and every idea that the agent has to do is just vertical video on your phone and then you can swipe left if you think it's a bad idea and you can swipe right if it's a good idea.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So the idea here is this agent is watching and listening to you, paying attention to the market, your users, and it's like, "Cool, here's something I should do."
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: No, this is a really good idea, but it reminds me, I had Dhanji on the podcast, CTO of Block, and they have this product called Goose, which is their own internal agent thing.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: That's just too annoying to do in the past, but now it's just totally worth the time of just getting an agent to go do something.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I think it's going to be much more like, "Okay, we've set up our coding agent to be really good at previewing the build or at running tests," maybe that's the first part that most people have set up.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And I think for some time at least, we're going to have humans curating which of these connectors or systems or components that the agent needs to be good at talking to.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And I think that, again, because you can use a coding agent for so many different things, it often is kind of broken in many sort of ways for specific behaviors.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: That would much more keep me in flow and enable the agent to take action on many more things.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And I think if you use Codex in that way, you'll just naturally start to understand the different ways of prompting it because it's a super powerful agent and model, but it is a little bit different to prompt Codex than other models.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so you still, at various phases, are going to want a human in the loop to help configure the coding agent to be effective.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And like you were talking about, it's like you can have an agent watch all the work you're doing, but if you don't have the agent also validating its work, then you're still bottlenecked on can you go review all that code?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So if we can rebuild systems to let the agent be default useful, we'll start unlocking hockey sticks.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: But now let's say, I don't know, you mentioned SAP, let's say you work in SAP, they have many complex systems and they're not going to be able to just get the agent to be self-sufficient overnight in those systems.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So they're going to have to slowly maybe replace systems or update systems to allow the agent to handle more of the work end to end.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I think it's actually a masterclass in building an agent that still leaves the human in control.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: 编码智能体 (coding agent)
entities.how_described: 本集把它说成：能自主编写代码、运行测试并执行任务的 AI 程序。它目前能极大加速开发，但当前的瓶颈在于人类审查和验证其生成代码的速度跟不上。

evidence.quote: Codex is OpenAI's coding agent.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so we're kind of getting to this idea where if you want to build any agent, maybe you should be building a coding agent.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Today, my guest is Alexander Embiricos, product lead for Codex, OpenAI's incredibly popular and powerful coding agent.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: And Codex is OpenAI's coding agent.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So we're kind of getting to this idea where, well, if you want to build any agent, maybe you should be building a coding agent and maybe to the user, a non-technical user, they won't even know they're using a coding agent, the same way that no one thinks about are they using the internet or not, which is they're more just like, "Is WiFi on?"
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I think there's two sides to it, but the one we were just talking about is this idea that maybe every agent should actually use code and be a coding agent.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I think, to give an example right now, nowadays when you work with a coding agent, it writes a ton of code, but it turns out writing code is actually one of the most fun parts of software engineering for many software engineers.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I think it's going to be much more like, "Okay, we've set up our coding agent to be really good at previewing the build or at running tests," maybe that's the first part that most people have set up.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And I think that, again, because you can use a coding agent for so many different things, it often is kind of broken in many sort of ways for specific behaviors.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so you still, at various phases, are going to want a human in the loop to help configure the coding agent to be effective.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: 沙箱 (sandbox)
entities.how_described: 本集把它说成：为了安全起见，让智能体在本地计算机内直接运行代码和命令的安全隔离环境，它能访问本地依赖且保证了系统安全。

evidence.quote: And it works within a sandbox, which is actually a really cool piece of tech to help that be safe and secure, but it has access to all those dependencies.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So if the agent needs to do something, it needs to run a command, it can do so within the sandbox.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And if it's a command that doesn't work in the sandbox, it can just ask you.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So the way that we built Codex is that it just uses the shell, but in order to make that safer and secure, we have a sandbox that the model is used to operating in.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: 上下文压缩 (compaction)
entities.how_described: 本集把它说成：为了让模型能够连续运行 24 小时而设计的一种动作，用以解决任务超出模型上下文窗口时丢失长程记忆的问题，需由模型、API 和外壳层三层配合完成。

evidence.quote: And so we have a solution for that, which we call compaction.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: But compaction is actually a feature that uses all three layers of that stack.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So you need to have a model that has a concept of compaction and knows like, "Okay, as I start to approach this context window, I might be asked to prepare to be run in a new context window."
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So shipping this compaction feature that now just made this behavior possible to anyone using Codex actually meant working across all three things.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: 外壳层 (harness)
entities.how_described: 本集把它说成：包裹在模型外部、控制智能体如何与计算机交互（如让其仅通过 Shell 终端工作）并准备有效载荷的执行外壳程序。

evidence.quote: One of the really cool things that we've seen is that the way that we decided to set up the Codex team was to build a really tightly integrated product and research team that are iterating on the model and the harness together.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so we were just training these models for use in our first party harness that we were very opinionated about.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: But then actually we need to serve that model through an API into a harness, and both of those things also have a really big role here.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And at the harness layer, you need a harness that can prepare the payload for this to be done.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And it's like, "Okay, now we've set it up so they can execute the build and it can see the results of its own changes, but we haven't yet built a good integration harness so that it can," in the case of Atlas... By the way, I don't know if they've done any of this or not.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: 压缩人才栈 (compressing the talent stack)
entities.how_described: 本集把它说成：因为 AI 补齐了技能短板，大幅削减了角色间的沟通成本，使得 PM 能直接做数据分析、设计师能直接开发原型。

evidence.quote: And Scott Belsky talks about this idea of compressing the talent stack.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And speaking of compressing the talent stack, I think our designers are very PME.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: 主动性 (proactivity)
entities.how_described: 本集把它说成：智能体的核心未来目标，即无需人类反复下达指令，就能主动参与项目规划、分析日志甚至修复线上问题。

evidence.quote: One of our major goals with Codex is to get to proactivity.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so we think of this as proactivity and one of our major goals with Codex is to get to proactivity.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: AGI
entities.how_described: 本集把它说成：OpenAI 正在狂奔的终极目标。嘉宾认为当系统能让智能体自我验证、默认提供有效帮助，从而解除对人类审查速度的依赖时，就能迎来触达 AGI 的生产力曲棍球棒式爆发。

evidence.quote: Being at OpenAI, I can't not ask about how far you think we are from AGI.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: Also, the 20x growth Codex is seeing right now and what they did to make it so good at coding, why his team is now focused on making it easier to review code, not just write code, his AGI timelines, his thoughts on when AI agents will actually be really useful, and so much more.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: I think this is critically important to achieve the mission of OpenAI, which is to deliver the benefits of AGI to all humanity.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: We have this strong sort of mission here at OpenAI basically to build AGI.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Being at OpenAI, I can't not ask about your AGI timeline and how far you think we are from AGI.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

entities.name: ChatGPT
